caseStudies.title: Lakehouse Migration
caseStudies.description: Consolidating data lake and warehouse into a unified platform.
caseStudies.overview: A financial services firm operated separate data lake and data warehouse infrastructure that had diverged over five years, resulting in duplicated datasets, inconsistent transformation logic, and a two-tier analyst experience where different teams worked from different versions of the truth.
caseStudies.problem: Data engineers spent significant time keeping the lake and warehouse in sync. Discrepancies between the two systems caused recurring conflicts in board-level reporting. The dual infrastructure had a combined annual cost exceeding $8M.
caseStudies.solution: A lakehouse migration was executed, consolidating all analytical workloads onto a single open-format platform. Existing ETL jobs were migrated and refactored to use declarative transformation frameworks. A medallion architecture was adopted to enforce data quality gates at each processing stage.

results.summary: The consolidated lakehouse eliminated the data consistency issues that had plagued the firm for years. Data engineers now focus on building new capabilities rather than maintaining synchronization between systems.

caseStudies.title: Feature Store for ML Platform
caseStudies.description: Centralised feature engineering to accelerate model development.
caseStudies.overview: A fintech company's data science teams were duplicating feature engineering work across projects, spending 60% of their time on data preparation rather than model development. Each team maintained its own feature pipelines with no sharing or reuse.
caseStudies.problem: Duplicated feature engineering led to subtle inconsistencies between models using nominally the same features. New model development required weeks of data preparation before experimentation could begin. Training-serving skew caused by different feature computation in training vs. production led to model performance degradation.
caseStudies.solution: A centralised feature store was built, housing over 500 production-grade features with consistent computation logic for both batch training and real-time serving. Feature discovery tooling allowed data scientists to browse and reuse existing features. Automated monitoring detected feature drift and training-serving skew in production.

stats.label: Time to first model experiment (down from 10)

results.summary: Data scientists now spend the majority of their time on model development rather than data preparation. The feature store has become the foundation of the company's ML platform, with new models benefiting from years of accumulated feature engineering work.

caseStudies.title: Real-Time Credit Risk Scoring
caseStudies.description: Millisecond-latency credit decisions at loan application scale.
caseStudies.overview: A digital lending platform needed to make real-time credit decisions within the loan application flow, requiring sub-100ms model inference latency at thousands of requests per second while maintaining the accuracy of models trained on rich historical features.
caseStudies.problem: The existing credit scoring process took 45 seconds, requiring applicants to wait for a decision. This friction caused significant drop-off during the application flow. The scoring models used features that were expensive to compute in real time, making direct productionisation of research models infeasible.
caseStudies.solution: A two-tier scoring architecture was designed: a fast real-time score using pre-computed features served from a low-latency store for the immediate decision, followed by an asynchronous enhanced score using richer features for downstream risk management. The feature store ensured consistency between training and serving environments.

results.summary: Applicants now receive instant credit decisions, eliminating the wait-time drop-off that had been a major conversion drag. The two-tier architecture delivers real-time performance without compromising model sophistication.
